body.title: Memory architecture: flat table + tree-as-index model
body.result: design doc: flat table schema, tree-as-index, O(1) ID lookup, phased migration plan, cache overlay recommendation
body.description:
Current model: memories live IN the tree (DNS-style — navigate the tree to reach the memory content). Proposed model: all memories in a flat table with stable IDs (<row_number>.<timestamp_based_id>); trees become pure indexing structures that carry weights and activation patterns but not content. Memories never move or change — only the trees update. Trees still calve the same way (when a branch becomes too specific it spawns a subtree); the calving updates the index, not the memory records.

Open design questions (to be answered in sprint discovery):
- Do we lose the hypothesized performance benefit of small trees? The original hypothesis was that small trees = less traversal cost. In the new model, the tree is still traversed for lookup but the content fetch is a direct row read. TBD whether this is net better or worse.
- Does the existing key/value caching overlay still make sense? With stable IDs and immutable records, caching is simpler but may still be valuable for hot paths.
- All existing memory references (esp. Igor's) must be updated to use the new ID format.

**Affected files:** TBD — discovery step. Likely: memory_palace table schema; Igor cortex traversal; Librarian palace_writer; all callers that navigate the tree to fetch content

**Design rules:** no-sqlite; Postgres for memory table; palace-first for tree structure; migration required (not a flag-day cutover)

**Scope boundary:** IN — flat table schema design; tree-as-index with weights; migration plan for existing memories; answer the performance question; caching overlay assessment. OUT — actual migration execution (separate ticket after design); emotional encoding changes

**Completion criteria:** (1) design doc answers the performance question with a benchmark or reasoned argument; (2) schema for flat table specified; (3) tree-as-index structure specified; (4) migration path from current to new described; (5) caching overlay recommendation stated
